vulnerabilities.description: BashTool allows arbitrary command execution via exec.CommandContext with user-controlled input. The #nosec G204 annotation disables security checks. While this is intentional for an agent system, any LLM prompt injection could lead to arbitrary shell commands on the host.

execSync with shell string containing pipes: 'ps aux 2>/dev/null | grep -c agentic-flow || echo "0"'. Although static, the pattern uses shell metacharacters and could be risky if modified to include user input.

execSync with complex shell string using awk and variable interpolation: 'ps aux | grep -E "(node|agentic|claude)" | grep -v grep | awk \'{sum += \$6} END {print int(sum/1024)}\''. Uses shell strings instead of execFileSync pattern.
execSync with shell string containing pipes and regex: 'ps aux 2>/dev/null | grep -c "claude-flow.*agent" || echo "0"'. Shell metacharacter injection risk if the pattern were user-controlled.
execSync('ps aux 2>/dev/null || echo ""', ...) - static string with shell:true implicit. Low risk as input is not user-controlled, but inconsistent with the execFileSync pattern used elsewhere.
safeExec function uses execSync with shell strings. Comment acknowledges this is a known pattern but the shell:true interpretation is implicit.

recommendations: Consider sanitizing bash tool input by restricting allowed commands or using a sandboxed shell (e.g., busybox ash, docker container) for the BashTool execution
Refactor statusline.js to use execFileSync with argument arrays instead of execSync with shell strings, following the pattern already implemented in getUserInfo() at line 52
Add input validation/allowlisting to BashTool to restrict which commands can be executed
The Go core (internal/) is well-structured with no hardcoded secrets, proper error handling, and the cobra dependency is up-to-date
No SQL injection risk - the scheduler uses in-memory storage with no external database
HTTP client has proper host allowlist validation preventing SSRF attacks
